threats.title: Registry onlyVaultMaintainer Modifier Uses Wrong Boolean Logic, Bricking Vault Administration
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyVaultMaintainer modifier uses OR (||) in the revert condition: `if (msg.sender != vaults[_vaultId].maintainer || hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender) == false)`. This means it only passes when the caller is BOTH the vault maintainer AND holds the EMERGENCY_ROLE. In contrast, the onlyVaultGoverner modifier correctly uses AND (&&). The same bug exists in onlyVaultMaintainerWithoutTimeLock. Functions gated by these modifiers (addConnector, removeTrustedPosition, updateConnectorTrustedTokens, addTrustedPosition) become inaccessible to their intended callers unless they hold both roles simultaneously.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable logic error that makes critical admin functions unreachable in normal operation, effectively bricking vault configuration. The maintainer address (a timelock contract) cannot add/remove connectors or update trusted tokens. Comparing to onlyVaultGoverner which uses && confirms the bug pattern.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != vaults[_vaultId].maintainer || hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender) == false) - uses || instead of &&; compare to onlyVaultGoverner at lines 47-50 which correctly uses &&

preconditions: Vault must be created with different maintainer and emergency addresses (normal operation)
Caller must be the vault maintainer but NOT also hold EMERGENCY_ROLE

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: OR logic in revert condition requires BOTH conditions to pass instead of EITHER
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: onlyVaultMaintainer: `msg.sender != maintainer || !hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE)` reverts unless both are true; should be `msg.sender != maintainer && !hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE)`
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No tests appear to verify that maintainer alone (without EMERGENCY_ROLE) can call gated functions

analysis_questions: Are there any deployment scripts that grant EMERGENCY_ROLE to the maintainer address?
Can the protocol function at all with this bug given the deployment configuration?
Does the onlyVaultMaintainerWithoutTimeLock have the identical bug?
validation_requirements: Call addConnector as vault maintainer without EMERGENCY_ROLE - should revert
Call addConnector as EMERGENCY_ROLE holder without being maintainer - should also revert
Compare behavior with onlyVaultGoverner which uses correct logic

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: No attacker needed - protocol is self-bricked. The vault maintainer (intended to be a timelock) cannot manage connectors, trusted tokens, or trusted positions unless it also holds EMERGENCY_ROLE. This prevents normal vault administration.
prerequisites: Vault deployed with separate maintainer and emergency addresses (standard deployment)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete inability to manage vault connectors and positions; new integrations impossible, compromised connectors cannot be removed
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All vault depositors - vault becomes unmanageable
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires redeployment of Registry contract or granting EMERGENCY_ROLE to maintainer (workaround)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for maintainer-only access (without emergency role) to gated functions

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily verifiable by calling any gated function as maintainer

threats.title: Holding Position Index Corruption via Swap-and-Pop Key Mismatch Between msg.sender and calculatorConnector
threats.hypothesis_description: When a holding position is removed via swap-and-pop in updateHoldingPosition, the moved element's isPositionUsed key is recalculated using `calculatorConnector` (field from the struct). However, when positions are created, the key uses `msg.sender` (the calling connector). For token positions (type 0), calculatorConnector is the accountingManager while msg.sender is the connector—these differ. This creates orphaned entries in the isPositionUsed mapping, corrupting the position tracking system.
threats.investigation_rationale: The key mismatch causes the position index lookup to return incorrect values after any swap-and-pop involving token positions. This corrupts the entire position tracking system, potentially creating duplicate positions, making positions unremovable, and eventually exhausting maxNumHoldingPositions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Creation uses keccak256(abi.encode(msg.sender, _positionId, _data)) but swap-and-pop at lines 221-228 recalculates key using keccak256(abi.encode(holdingPositions[positionIndex].calculatorConnector, positionId, data))

preconditions: At least two holding positions exist in a vault
One position is removed triggering swap-and-pop (not the last element)
The moved position has calculatorConnector != the connector that created it (always true for token positions type 0)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Key derivation inconsistency: creation at line 213 uses msg.sender, swap-and-pop at line 222-226 uses calculatorConnector
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: For _updateTokenInRegistry, calculatorConnector is accountingManager (from calculatePositionId) but msg.sender creating the position is the connector itself
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that the key reconstruction matches the original key

analysis_questions: How frequently does swap-and-pop actually occur in practice?
Which position types have calculatorConnector != creating connector?
Can the corruption cascade to block all position operations?
validation_requirements: Create multiple token positions from different connectors
Remove a position that is not the last element
Verify isPositionUsed mapping consistency after removal
Attempt to find/remove the moved position - should fail due to wrong key

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: During normal vault operations, when connectors add/remove token positions, the swap-and-pop corrupts the index mapping. Over time, phantom entries accumulate, the maxNumHoldingPositions limit is hit with fewer real positions than expected, and some positions become permanently stuck.
prerequisites: Vault with multiple active token holding positions
Normal removal of any non-last position

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Position tracking corruption leads to inability to remove positions, potential duplicate entries, and eventual vault freeze when maxNumHoldingPositions is exhausted
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Extremely difficult - requires manual reconstruction of position mappings or contract upgrade

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for position index consistency after swap-and-pop with heterogeneous position types

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Occurs during normal operations whenever positions are removed
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Can be detected by comparing isPositionUsed mapping with actual array contents

threats.title: TVL Calculation Underflow DoS in Multiple Connectors Freezes Entire Vault
threats.hypothesis_description: Multiple connector _getPositionTVL functions perform unchecked subtraction of debt from collateral (e.g., `totalCollateral - totalDebt`). In Solidity 0.8.x, this reverts on underflow if debt exceeds collateral. Since TVLHelper.getTVL iterates ALL positions and calls each connector's getPositionTVL, a single underwater position reverts the entire TVL calculation, blocking all vault deposits, withdrawals, and fee calculations that depend on TVL.
threats.investigation_rationale: Market conditions can easily push leveraged positions underwater (interest accrual, price drops, liquidation). The TVLHelper itself handles negative total TVL gracefully (returns 0), but individual connector TVL functions do not. This is a systemic DoS vector affecting any vault with lending positions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: tvl = totalDepositAmount - totalBAmount; unchecked subtraction reverts if totalBAmount > totalDepositAmount. Same pattern in PrismaConnector:160, CompoundConnector:108, Dolomite:91, AaveConnector:103

preconditions: Vault has at least one lending position (Silo, Prisma, Compound, Dolomite, or Aave)
The position becomes underwater (debt > collateral) due to market conditions, interest accrual, or partial liquidation

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: SiloConnector.sol line 103, PrismaConnector.sol line ~160, CompoundConnector.sol line ~108, Dolomite.sol line ~91, AaveConnector.sol line ~103
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unchecked subtraction: collateral - debt without underflow protection
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Solidity 0.8.x reverts on underflow, propagating revert through TVLHelper.getTVL to all vault operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check for debt > collateral before subtraction; should return 0 in that case

analysis_questions: Which vault operations depend on TVLHelper.getTVL?
Can the underwater position be removed while TVL calculation is broken?
Is there an emergency bypass for TVL calculation?
validation_requirements: Create a lending position, then simulate market conditions where debt > collateral
Call TVLHelper.getTVL and confirm it reverts
Confirm that deposits and withdrawals are blocked

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: During market volatility, a vault's Aave/Compound/Silo position goes underwater. TVLHelper.getTVL reverts, freezing all deposits, withdrawals, and share price calculations. The vault is permanently locked until the position is manually brought above water, which may be impossible if the keeper cannot execute transactions due to the TVL calculation failure.
prerequisites: Active lending position in any of the affected connectors

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete vault freeze - no deposits or withdrawals possible, potentially trapping all user funds
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires external capital injection to bring position above water, or emergency action to remove the underwater position

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests for underwater position TVL calculation behavior

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None for attack; recovery requires capital
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Triggered by market conditions, not attacker action
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring health factors of lending positions

threats.title: SwapAndBridgeHandler onlyExistingRoute Modifier Allows Disabled Routes to Execute
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyExistingRoute modifier uses AND (&&) logic: `if (routes[_routeId].route == address(0) && !routes[_routeId].isEnabled) revert`. This only reverts when the route address is ZERO AND the route is disabled. A disabled route with a non-zero address passes the check. Since executeSwap and executeBridge never independently verify isEnabled, the emergency disable mechanism (setEnableRoute) is ineffective.
threats.investigation_rationale: If a route is compromised or deprecated, the maintainer/emergency role disables it via setEnableRoute. But the disabled route can still be used for swaps and bridges because the modifier logic is wrong. This bypasses a critical safety mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (routes[_routeId].route == address(0) && !routes[_routeId].isEnabled) revert RouteNotFound(); - uses && instead of ||, allowing disabled routes with non-zero addresses to pass

preconditions: A route exists with a non-zero address
The route has been disabled via setEnableRoute(routeId, false)
An eligible user attempts to use the disabled route

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: AND logic in condition should be OR: route == address(0) || !isEnabled
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Disabled routes remain functional, defeating the emergency disable mechanism
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No secondary isEnabled check in executeSwap or executeBridge

analysis_questions: Are there scenarios where a route needs to be emergency-disabled?
Can a disabled route be exploited to drain funds?
Does verifyRoute also have this bug?
validation_requirements: Add a route, disable it, then attempt executeSwap - should fail but won't
Verify that setEnableRoute(routeId, false) has no practical effect

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A swap route is found to have a vulnerability or is compromised. The emergency role disables it via setEnableRoute. However, eligible users (connectors) can still execute swaps through the disabled route because the modifier doesn't properly check the isEnabled flag. Funds routed through the compromised route are at risk.
prerequisites: At least one route added with a non-zero address

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Emergency disable mechanism is ineffective; compromised routes remain usable, potentially draining swap funds
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any vault using the handler for swaps/bridges
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Must remove the route entirely or set its address to address(0)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for disabled route rejection

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Can be verified by attempting swap on disabled route

threats.title: DolomiteConnector.openBorrowPosition Passes removePosition=true Instead of false, Losing Position Tracking
threats.hypothesis_description: DolomiteConnector.openBorrowPosition calls `registry.updateHoldingPosition(vaultId, ..., abi.encode(accountId), "", true)` with `removePosition=true`. This attempts to REMOVE a position when the intent is to ADD one. Since the position doesn't exist yet (first call returns positionIndex=0), the removal is a no-op (returns type(uint256).max). The borrow position is never tracked in the registry.
threats.investigation_rationale: The wrong boolean flag causes borrow positions to be invisible to the TVL calculation. Borrowed amounts and associated risks are not tracked, leading to incorrect TVL and potential hidden leverage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: registry.updateHoldingPosition(vaultId, registry.calculatePositionId(address(this), DOL_POSITION_ID, ""), abi.encode(accountId), "", true); - passes true (remove) instead of false (add)

preconditions: Manager calls openBorrowPosition on DolomiteConnector

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Compare with deposit() at line 33 which correctly passes false; compare with closeBorrowPosition which also passes true (correct for closing)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that position was actually registered after creation

analysis_questions: Does the missing position tracking affect TVL calculations?
Can the borrow position ever be properly closed if it was never tracked?
Are there other boolean flag errors in the codebase?
validation_requirements: Call openBorrowPosition and verify registry state
Check if _getPositionTVL accounts for the borrow position
Verify closeBorrowPosition behavior when position was never added

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Manager opens a Dolomite borrow position. The position is not tracked in the registry, so TVL calculation misses it. If the borrow creates debt, the vault's TVL is overstated (debt not subtracted). This leads to inflated share prices, allowing withdrawals of more value than the vault actually holds.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Untracked debt leads to overstated TVL, enabling excess withdrawals that drain the vault
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors - remaining depositors bear the untracked debt
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code fix; position may need manual registry insertion

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for position registry state after openBorrowPosition

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Inspect holdingPositions array after openBorrowPosition call

threats.title: SNXv3Connector TVL Double-Counts Collateral by Adding totalDeposited and totalAssigned
threats.hypothesis_description: SNXv3Connector._getPositionTVL calculates TVL as `_getValue(collateralType, base, totalDeposited + totalAssigned)`. In SNX V3, `getAccountCollateral` returns (totalDeposited, totalAssigned, totalLocked) where totalDeposited is the total collateral in the account and totalAssigned is the portion delegated to pools (a subset of totalDeposited). Adding them double-counts the delegated portion.
threats.investigation_rationale: If totalAssigned is a subset of totalDeposited (as documented in SNX V3), the TVL inflation is exactly totalAssigned. This inflates share prices proportionally to delegated collateral.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (uint256 totalDeposited, uint256 totalAssigned, uint256 totalLocked) = SNXCoreProxy.getAccountCollateral(accountId, collateralType); tvl = _getValue(collateralType, base, totalDeposited + totalAssigned);

preconditions: SNXV3Connector has deposited and delegated collateral
totalAssigned > 0 (collateral delegated to a pool)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: totalDeposited + totalAssigned where totalAssigned is a subset of totalDeposited
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Should use just totalDeposited, or totalDeposited + unrealized rewards if applicable
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No documentation of SNX V3 getAccountCollateral return value semantics in the code

analysis_questions: Confirm SNX V3 getAccountCollateral semantics: is totalAssigned a subset of totalDeposited?
What is the magnitude of overcounting for typical delegated positions?
Does totalLocked need to be considered?
validation_requirements: Deposit and delegate via SNX V3, then compare getAccountCollateral values
Verify if totalDeposited >= totalAssigned always holds
Compare connector TVL with actual position value

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Vault deposits and delegates collateral in SNX V3. TVL is inflated by the delegated amount. Share price is artificially high, allowing existing holders to withdraw more than their fair share.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: TVL inflated by up to 2x the delegated collateral amount, enabling excess withdrawals
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: New depositors receive fewer shares; existing depositors can extract excess value
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code fix to use correct TVL formula

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test comparing connector TVL with actual SNX V3 position value

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare getAccountCollateral values against known deposit amounts

threats.title: Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity Empty Implementation Provides Zero Withdrawal Verification
threats.hypothesis_description: Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity is declared as `function verifyRemoveLiquidity(uint256 withdrawAmount, uint256 sentAmount, bytes memory data) external view { }` with an empty body. This function is called by BaseConnector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress when the accounting manager requests token transfers. The Watcher security layer, designed to validate withdrawal amounts and prevent unauthorized fund extraction, provides no actual verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Watcher role is documented as a critical safety mechanism to monitor and revert suspicious actions. With an empty verification function, any withdrawal amount requested by the accounting manager passes without scrutiny. The entire safety layer designed to prevent malicious or erroneous withdrawals is absent.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function verifyRemoveLiquidity(uint256 withdrawAmount, uint256 sentAmount, bytes memory data) external view { } - empty function body

preconditions: Accounting manager calls sendTokensToTrustedAddress on any connector
The watcher contract is the deployed Watchers.sol (which has the empty function)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Empty function body for security-critical verification
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: sendTokensToTrustedAddress at BaseConnector.sol trusts the newAmount from data parameter without any watcher validation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No comparison between withdrawAmount and sentAmount, no bounds checking, no signature verification

analysis_questions: Was this intended as a placeholder for future implementation?
Are there other safety mechanisms that compensate for the missing verification?
Could the Watchers contract be a multisig that verifies off-chain?
validation_requirements: Trace the sendTokensToTrustedAddress accounting manager path
Verify that newAmount from data parameter is used without bounds checking
Confirm no other verification occurs

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised or buggy accounting manager calls sendTokensToTrustedAddress with an inflated newAmount in the data parameter. The Watcher verification does nothing, and the connector transfers the requested amount. The attacker drains connector funds through the accounting manager path.
prerequisites: Compromised accounting manager or keeper that can trigger withdrawals

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: The documented safety mechanism for withdrawal verification is completely absent; any withdrawal amount passes
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors - funds can be extracted without verification
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires implementing actual verification logic in the Watchers contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test verifying that watcher actually validates withdrawal amounts

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Applicable to every withdrawal through accounting manager
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Code inspection immediately reveals the empty function

threats.title: BaseConnector.transferPositionToAnotherConnector Silently Succeeds When Target Connector Is Inactive
threats.hypothesis_description: transferPositionToAnotherConnector checks `if (registry.isAnActiveConnector(vaultId, connector))` but does nothing when the check fails—no revert, no error. The function silently succeeds, and the keeper believes the position was transferred successfully when in reality nothing happened. Token amounts are not moved, but the operation is considered complete.
threats.investigation_rationale: Silent failures in critical financial operations are dangerous. If a connector is deactivated between the time a strategy is planned and executed, the position transfer silently fails. The keeper's strategy assumptions become invalid, potentially leading to incorrect risk management or stranded positions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (registry.isAnActiveConnector(vaultId, connector)) { IConnector(connector).addLiquidity(tokens, amounts, data); } - no else branch or revert for inactive connector

preconditions: Target connector is inactive (disabled in registry)
Manager calls transferPositionToAnotherConnector

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: if-without-else on critical operation; no revert on failure path
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Function emits TransferPositionToConnector event regardless of whether transfer actually happened

analysis_questions: Can a deactivated connector cause strategy inconsistencies?
Does the keeper rely on the success of this function for subsequent operations?
Are there compensating mechanisms to detect failed transfers?
validation_requirements: Call transferPositionToAnotherConnector with an inactive connector
Verify function succeeds without reverting
Verify no tokens were actually moved

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A connector is deactivated as part of emergency response. The keeper then attempts to rebalance positions by transferring to this connector. The call succeeds silently, tokens remain in the source connector, but the strategy assumes they've moved. Subsequent operations based on this assumption may fail or produce incorrect results.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Strategy execution becomes unreliable; positions may be stranded or double-counted in strategy logic
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Vault depositors affected by incorrect strategy execution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Detectable through balance monitoring; recoverable by re-executing transfers

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for inactive connector transfer behavior

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare pre/post balances to detect silent failure

threats.title: BalancerFlashLoan.makeFlashLoan is publicly callable, enabling griefing and potential vault-impacting external calls via keeper-only check bypass attempts
threats.hypothesis_description: The flash loan entrypoint `makeFlashLoan` is permissionless and writes `caller = msg.sender` before initiating the Balancer flashLoan callback. Although `receiveFlashLoan` later checks `caller == keeperContract`, any unprivileged caller can repeatedly trigger flash loan attempts that will revert in `receiveFlashLoan` after Balancer transfers, consuming resources and potentially causing operational disruption. Additionally, because `receiveFlashLoan` does not validate destinationConnector targets, a future bug or bypass in the keeper check could become catastrophic.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even with trusted sequencer/no mempool, unprivileged spamming is feasible. If Balancer flashLoan mechanics charge fees or impose rate limits, repeated reverting loans could degrade system availability or cause unexpected costs. The public surface combined with stateful `caller` is a common footgun worth validating under Balancer semantics.
deployment_assumptions: Balancer Vault allows flashLoan calls from any contract regardless of caller, and failed callbacks still consume significant gas/fees or cause rate-limiting
Protocol operations rely on BalancerFlashLoan availability in time-sensitive keeper flows
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence of access restriction on BalancerFlashLoan.makeFlashLoan; function is `external` without auth in BalancerFlashLoan.sol.

inventory_refs.function: makeFlashLoan(IERC20[] memory tokens, uint256[] memory amounts, bytes memory userData)

proof_of_signal.pattern: Public `makeFlashLoan` sets state variable `caller = msg.sender` and calls `vault.flashLoan(...)` with no access control; only later does `receiveFlashLoan` gate via `caller == keeperContract`.

preconditions: Attacker can call BalancerFlashLoan.makeFlashLoan on-chain
Balancer flashLoan attempt reaches callback and reverts after keeper check fails
Protocol is sensitive to griefing / repeated failures (e.g., keeper automation, monitoring, gas budgets)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: permissionless flashloan initiation with stateful caller tracking
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: griefing or operational disruption by repeated reverting flashloans
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No onlyKeeper/onlyOwner guard; no rate limiting; no early vaultId/keeper validation before calling Balancer

analysis_questions: Does Balancer charge fees or enforce penalties when callback reverts?
Can repeated failed flash loans interfere with keeper executions (e.g., nonce/state assumptions elsewhere)?
Is `caller` used safely under nested flashloan scenarios (reentrancy is blocked by nonReentrant in makeFlashLoan but confirm Balancer behavior)?
validation_requirements: Fork-test BalancerVault flashLoan with reverting callback and quantify gas/fee side-effects
Attempt spam calls to measure practical chain-level impact and any protocol monitoring side-effects
Confirm no unexpected persistence of `caller` if flashLoan reverts at different points

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker repeatedly calls makeFlashLoan with arbitrary userData. receiveFlashLoan reverts at Unauthorized(caller). Even if funds are safe, the attacker can consume keeper/operator resources and potentially degrade availability if Balancer/infra imposes limits or if the protocol assumes flashloan endpoint is reliable.
prerequisites: Sufficient gas budget for repeated calls

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Primarily operational DoS; possible external fee leakage depending on Balancer config
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All vaults relying on flashloan-based strategy steps
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Mitigate by adding access control or disabling flashloan usage

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for unauthorized makeFlashLoan callers and Balancer-side fee behavior on revert

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Quantify revert-path gas and any Balancer fee side-effects
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal (gas only) unless Balancer requires prefunding
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor contract events MakeFlashLoan/ReceiveFlashLoan for spam patterns

threats.title: LZHelperSender.updateTVL uses entire contract ETH balance as LayerZero fee, enabling accidental or adversarial ETH burn/drain via donations
threats.hypothesis_description: LZHelperSender.updateTVL sets MessagingFee(nativeFee=address(this).balance). Because the contract has a payable receive() function, anyone can donate ETH; the next updateTVL will spend the full balance as fees. This can either burn treasury ETH or cause unpredictable fee payment behavior and potential message failures (if the fee must match exact quoting behavior).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code path using full balance. It is exploitable without any privileged access: attackers can force the protocol to overpay fees (economic griefing) by donating ETH, or cause operational uncertainty about fee budgeting.

inventory_refs.function: updateTVL(uint256 vaultId, uint256 tvl, uint256 updateTime)

proof_of_signal.pattern: _lzSend(..., MessagingFee(address(this).balance, 0), ...) uses entire ETH balance as fee; contract has `receive() external payable {}`.

preconditions: LZHelperSender holds ETH (either funded by protocol or donated by third parties)
An authorized omniChainManager calls updateTVL (normal operation)
LayerZero endpoint accepts the provided fee and consumes it

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: uses full contract balance for fee
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: unbounded fee payment and griefable economics
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No fee quoting, no max fee cap, no refund management beyond payable(this)

analysis_questions: Does LayerZero refund unused native fee to the specified refund address (here: address(this))? If so, does it still create accounting/availability issues?
Can an attacker force meaningful loss (e.g., if endpoint consumes full fee)?
Is there any owner function to sweep ETH from LZHelperSender (not shown) to manage balances?
validation_requirements: Review LayerZero OApp v2 fee semantics for overpayment and refunds
Fork-test updateTVL with donated ETH and measure net ETH spent vs refunded
Check operational scripts for how fees are intended to be supplied

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker donates ETH to LZHelperSender. Next TVL update spends the entire ETH as message fee, potentially burning protocol funds or creating unpredictable fee usage. Repeated donations force repeated overpayment/griefing.
prerequisites: Attacker has ETH to donate (can be small if causing operational confusion rather than loss)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Economic griefing; potential fee overpayment / treasury loss depending on LZ refund rules
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users relying on cross-chain TVL sync correctness/availability
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Code change to use quoted fee and cap; add sweep function

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests around fee payment logic and donation/grief scenarios

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor incoming ETH donations and subsequent fee spending

threats.title: UNIv3 TVL Overcounting via Aggregate Pool Position Query Enables Share Price Inflation
threats.hypothesis_description: UNIv3Connector._getPositionTVL queries pool.positions(key) using keccak256(positionManager, tickLower, tickUpper), which returns the AGGREGATE liquidity for ALL NonfungiblePositionManager NFTs at those ticks, not just the vault's specific tokenId. Any external user's liquidity at the same tick range through the same positionManager inflates the vault's reported TVL. An attacker can deposit into the vault, add external liquidity at the same tick range, inflate TVL, then withdraw vault shares at the inflated price.
threats.investigation_rationale: The pool.positions(key) aggregation is a fundamental accounting error. The correct approach uses positionManager.positions(tokenId) for per-NFT liquidity. Since Uniswap V3 positions at popular tick ranges are common, this is always exploitable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.positions(keccak256(positionManager, tL, tU)) returns aggregate liquidity, not per-tokenId liquidity

preconditions: Vault has at least one UNIv3 LP position
Attacker can add liquidity to same pool/tick range via the same NonfungiblePositionManager
AccountingManager uses TVL for share pricing

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: pool.positions(key) returns ALL liquidity from positionManager at ticks, not just vault's tokenId
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker adds external liquidity at same tick range to inflate vault TVL

economic_questions: What is the typical ratio of vault liquidity to total liquidity at a tick range?
How much capital is needed to significantly inflate the TVL?
Can the attack be done atomically with a flash loan?
validation_requirements: Verify pool.positions(key) vs positionManager.positions(tokenId) output difference
Calculate capital needed to double apparent TVL
Test end-to-end: deposit, inflate, withdraw cycle

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Equal to vault's UNI position size to 2x inflate (flash loanable)

threats.title: SiloConnector TVL Miscalculation via Hardcoded 18-Decimal Price Normalization
threats.hypothesis_description: SiloConnector._getPositionTVL normalizes prices using _getValue(asset, base, 1e18), assuming all tokens have 18 decimals. For USDC (6 decimals), this queries the price of 1e18 raw units (= 1e12 USDC = $1 trillion). The balance (in 6 decimal native units) is then multiplied by this inflated price and divided by 1e18, producing vastly incorrect TVL values. This leads to share price distortion enabling profit extraction.
threats.investigation_rationale: USDC is one of the most common DeFi tokens with 6 decimals. Deployment scripts show USDC as base token. Silo markets commonly include USDC. The miscalculation is mathematically provable.
deployment_assumptions: Silo positions involve non-18-decimal tokens (e.g., USDC, USDT, WBTC)
threats.config_evidence: deployAccountingManager.ts uses tokens['USDC'] as baseToken; USDC has 6 decimals

proof_of_signal.pattern: _getValue(assets[i], base, 1e18) hardcodes 1e18 regardless of token decimals; depositAmount * price / 1e18 produces wrong result for 6-decimal tokens

preconditions: SiloConnector holds positions in non-18-decimal tokens
USDC or USDT is used in Silo markets
Value oracle returns valid prices for the requested amounts

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: For USDC: price = _getValue(USDC, base, 1e18) gets price of 1e12 USDC; balance(1000e6) * price / 1e18 is wildly wrong
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Automatic miscalculation inflates or deflates TVL for non-18-decimal tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If TVL is inflated, existing depositors extract excess value; if deflated, new depositors get shares too cheaply

economic_questions: What is the exact TVL distortion factor for USDC (6 decimals)?
Does the oracle handle 1e18 units of a 6-decimal token correctly or revert?
Which direction does the error favor (inflation or deflation)?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact TVL output for 1000 USDC deposit in Silo
Compare against correct TVL calculation
Test with actual oracle behavior for extreme amounts

threats.title: Cross-Chain TVL Update Lacks Timestamp Monotonicity Check Enabling Stale Data Injection
threats.hypothesis_description: OmnichainManagerBaseChain.updateTVL accepts any updateTime without checking if it's newer than the existing positionTimestamp. If LayerZero delivers messages out of order, a stale TVL value can overwrite a more recent one. An attacker could exploit windows where stale (lower) TVL is recorded to deposit at cheap share prices, then trigger a fresh TVL update to profit from the correction.
threats.investigation_rationale: LayerZero does not guarantee in-order delivery. Without monotonicity checks, the most recent TVL data can be replaced by older data, creating share price manipulation windows.

threats.title: SwapAndBridgeHandler Has No Function to Remove Eligible Users, Creating Irrevocable Access
threats.hypothesis_description: The SwapAndBridgeHandler contract has addEligibleUser but no corresponding removeEligibleUser function. threats.title: Keepers multisig execution can be griefed by replaying signatures after intentional revert to consume signer bandwidth (nonce increments pre-call)
threats.hypothesis_description: Keepers.execute() increments nonce before performing the external call and reverts if the call fails, which rolls back nonce increment. An attacker who can induce the destination call to revert deterministically (e.g., via protocol state) can force repeated signing attempts for the same nonce/tx by keepers, consuming operational bandwidth. While unprivileged attackers cannot call execute() unless they are an owner, the realistic vector is a partially compromised owner key or a misconfigured owner set (still operationally plausible), making this a medium-realism ops DoS.
threats.investigation_rationale: Multi-sig operations often fail due to external protocol states; if the signing process is expensive (human/ops), repeated failures can delay critical responses. This is not a direct fund loss but can block time-sensitive rebalances/liquidation prevention.
deployment_assumptions: At least one Keepers owner key/address can submit execute() calls that other owners co-sign, or owners sign without independent simulation
Operational signing flow does not always simulate call success on-chain state before collecting signatures
threats.config_evidence: No explicit deployment evidence of keeper signing SOPs in provided scripts; Keepers is present but not wired in scripts shown.
affected_functions: execute(address destination, bytes data, uint256 gasLimit, address executor, bytes32[] sigR, bytes32[] sigS, uint8[] sigV, uint256 deadline)

Destination call can be made to revert deterministically under some on-chain condition

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Nonce is tied to signed payload; revert causes nonce to remain same, enabling repeated signing for same tx hash with no progress.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner participation (partial compromise or poor process).

threats.title: Omnichain bridge transaction approvals never expire, allowing delayed execution under changed conditions
threats.hypothesis_description: OmnichainLogic.updateBridgeTransactionApproval() records an approval timestamp, and startBridgeTransaction() only checks that the waiting time has passed; there is no upper bound expiry. If an approval is granted and later the environment changes (routes compromised, destination address rotated, market moves), the previously-approved bridgeRequest can still be executed at any point in the future as long as its hash matches. This increases operational risk and could be exploited if an attacker can cause managers to approve a transaction hash and later trigger execution when it becomes harmful (still on-chain timing, but requires access to onlyManager).
threats.investigation_rationale: Non-expiring approvals are a known operational hazard for delayed execution systems; the risk is not purely theoretical because cross-chain ops often involve delays and coordination, and old approvals can resurface.
deployment_assumptions: Managers use updateBridgeTransactionApproval in practice
BridgeRequest hash collisions are not feasible; risk is about reuse of the same approved request later
OnlyManager actors might execute stale approvals inadvertently or under partial compromise
threats.config_evidence: No deployment scripts show omnichain manager wiring; approval/execution flow described in code and Phase A DF-003.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (approvedBridgeTXN[txn]==0 || approvedBridgeTXN[txn]+WAITING_TIME>block.timestamp) revert; // no max validity window

preconditions: A bridge transaction hash has been approved (approvedBridgeTXN[txn] set)
OnlyManager can later call startBridgeTransaction with the same BridgeRequest
Sufficient time has passed (> BRIDGE_TXN_WAITING_TIME)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Two-step approval intended as safety delay, but lack of expiry turns it into a perpetual authorization.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Indefinite after the waiting time passes

operational_questions: Do Watchers/ops treat approvals as one-time intents with assumed short validity?
Is there off-chain tracking to invalidate old approvals?
Can approvedBridgeTXN be cleared operationally if not executed (it can be toggled off, but requires action)?
validation_requirements: Review ops workflow for bridge approvals and cancellations
Test that old approvals remain executable weeks later

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Bridge executes using current route configuration

threats.title: PancakeswapConnector can orphan TVL tracking when NFT is transferred to MasterChef without corresponding registry holding position ownership update
threats.hypothesis_description: PancakeswapConnector.sendPositionToMasterChef() transfers the Uniswap V3 position NFT from the connector to MasterChef. If UNIv3Connector-based TVL/accounting assumes the connector holds the NFT (e.g., for collect/burn operations or position lookups), transferring it away may break subsequent lifecycle management or registry position consistency (position remains recorded but asset is custodized elsewhere). This can cause incorrect TVL, inability to decrease/burn/collect, and operational stuck states. Exploitability is via manager actions; however, it can become an externally-triggered issue if MasterChef behaves unexpectedly or the NFT cannot be withdrawn.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical operational failure mode: moving custody to third party requires corresponding accounting changes and robust withdrawal paths. If the position becomes stuck, funds can be effectively frozen.
deployment_assumptions: Vault uses PancakeswapConnector with MasterChef staking of V3 NFTs
Registry holding positions for UNI V3 are used for TVL and for determining removability
MasterChef withdrawal path can fail under certain conditions (e.g., rewards contract changes)
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence of Pancakeswap usage in scripts shown beyond generic connector deploy; MasterChef address is passed at deploy-time.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC721(positionManager).safeTransferFrom(address(this), address(masterchef), tokenId); // no registry update or custody state recorded

preconditions: A UNIv3 position exists and is tracked in registry as holding position
Manager calls sendPositionToMasterChef and transfers NFT away
Subsequent management/TVL logic expects NFT in connector custody or relies on positionManager.positions(tokenId)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Custody transfer is not mirrored in registry state; no explicit staking-position holding position type exists.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires onlyManager call, but can lead to stuck states independent of attacker intent.

Are there safeguards preventing staking positions that are needed for emergency unwind?

Verify registry holding positions correctly reflect staked positions

threats.title: Lido withdrawal request tracking can become inconsistent if stETH is rebasing or multiple requests exist (single positionId used)
threats.hypothesis_description: LidoConnector.requestWithdrawals stores a single holding position keyed by calculatePositionId(address(this), LIDO_WITHDRAWAL_REQUEST_ID, ""). It writes requestId in p.data and requested amount in p.additionalData, but does not support multiple concurrent withdrawal requests (same positionId/data key). Subsequent requests may overwrite/alias tracking, leading to incorrect TVL reporting, inability to properly clear positions, or mismatched claimWithdrawal calls. Additionally, additionalData caches amount at request time while stETH is rebasing, creating drift between tracked amount and eventual redeemed amount/value.
threats.investigation_rationale: Withdrawal queues commonly require multiple in-flight requests. Incorrect tracking can cause operational errors and mispricing even without an active attacker.
deployment_assumptions: Vault strategy may create multiple Lido withdrawal requests over time without fully settling prior ones
AccountingManager relies on registry holdings for TVL/share pricing
stETH rebasing behavior affects requested vs realized amounts/value over time
threats.config_evidence: Token semantics in Phase A note stETH rebasing; contract code stores only one request positionId with empty data.

preconditions: Lido withdrawal functionality is used
More than one withdrawal request can be created before prior request is claimed/removed
Registry position uniqueness relies on (msg.sender, positionId, data) with data empty for this position

initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: OnlyManager executes, but the failure mode can occur during normal operations.

threats.title: Cross-Chain TVL Staleness Checks Defeated by Default Timestamps
threats.hypothesis_description: TVLHelper.getLatestUpdateTime returns type(uint256).max if positions are initialized with the default timestamp, defeating any staleness checks for cross-chain TVL data.

threats.title: SiloConnector.borrow Missing Health Factor Check Allows Unsafe Borrowing Leading to Liquidation
threats.hypothesis_description: SiloConnector.borrow() does not check solvency/health factor after borrowing, unlike every other lending connector (Aave, Compound, Prisma) and unlike SiloConnector's own withdraw() and repay() functions. This allows the position to drop below minimumHealthFactor, making it eligible for external liquidation.
threats.investigation_rationale: Asymmetric safety check: withdraw checks isSolvent, repay checks isSolvent, but borrow has zero post-execution validation. The SiloConnector constructor explicitly sets MINIMUM_HEALTH_FACTOR = 5e17, proving intent to enforce health bounds. Keeper strategy execution could accidentally over-borrow, and the contract fails to prevent it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: silo.borrow() called without subsequent SolvencyV2.isSolvent() check, unlike withdraw (line 95) and repay (line 104)

preconditions: SiloConnector has an active deposit position in a Silo market
Keeper calls borrow with an amount exceeding safe health factor bounds
Silo protocol allows the borrow to proceed on its end

threats.title: GearBoxV3._getPositionTVL Uses Magic address(840) for USD Pricing, Likely Causing TVL Revert and Vault DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: GearBoxV3._getPositionTVL calls _getValue(address(840), base, amount) using address(840) (0x348) as a pseudo-token address for USD denomination. If the Noya Value Oracle has no price feed mapping for address(840) -> baseToken, the call reverts, causing TVLHelper.getTVL to revert and DoS-ing the entire vault.
threats.investigation_rationale: address(840) is a non-standard magic number for USD. No deployment script configures the value oracle with a price feed for this address. The Noya Value Oracle would need an explicit mapping from address(0x348) to the vault's baseToken, which is a fragile and undocumented requirement.
deployment_assumptions: Value oracle must have a configured price feed for address(840) to baseToken
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script in the provided set configures a price feed for address(840). deploySharedContracts.ts creates NoyaValueOracle but doesn't show oracle feed configuration.

threats.title: LidoConnector._getPositionTVL Uses Stale Cached stETH Amount, Diverging from Actual Rebased Value
threats.hypothesis_description: LidoConnector stores the withdrawal request amount in additionalData at request time and uses this cached value for TVL calculation. Since stETH is a rebasing token that accrues staking rewards, the actual value of the pending withdrawal grows over time while the cached TVL value remains static, leading to systematic TVL undercounting.
threats.investigation_rationale: The cached amount diverges from reality by the stETH rebase rate (~3-5% APY). For long-pending withdrawal requests, this creates material TVL miscalculation affecting share price, deposit amounts, and withdrawal amounts for all vault participants.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.decode(p.additionalData, (uint256)) returns cached amount from request time, not current rebased value

preconditions: LidoConnector has pending withdrawal requests
stETH has rebased since request was made (always true over time)
Withdrawal requests remain pending for meaningful duration

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Systematic undercounting: new depositors get more shares than deserved at suppressed share price
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: At 4% APY, a 30-day pending request creates ~0.33% TVL undercount; attacker deposits at suppressed price, claims gains when withdrawal finalizes

threats.title: LifiImplementation._forward Leaves Residual ERC20 Allowance to LiFi Router After Swap
threats.hypothesis_description: LifiImplementation._forward sets a token allowance to the LiFi router via forceApprove but never revokes it after the swap completes. If LiFi uses less than the approved amount (partial fill), or if the swap data is crafted to not consume the full amount, a residual allowance persists allowing the LiFi router to spend remaining tokens from the LifiImplementation contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: GearBoxV3.executeCommands explicitly revokes approval after operations (_revokeApproval), showing the developers are aware of this pattern. LifiImplementation lacks this cleanup step. Tokens remaining in LifiImplementation after a partial swap are at risk.

threats.title: StargateConnector.withdrawFromStargatePool Truncates poolId from uint256 to uint16, Potentially Targeting Wrong Pool
threats.hypothesis_description: StargateConnector.withdrawFromStargatePool casts poolId from uint256 to uint16 when calling stargateRouter.instantRedeemLocal. For poolIds >= 65536, the truncation maps to a different pool, causing the withdrawal to interact with the wrong Stargate pool and potentially losing funds.

threats.title: Registry.addVault Missing Zero Address Check for Emergency and MaintainerWithoutTimelock Addresses
threats.hypothesis_description: Registry.addVault requires non-zero addresses for governer, accountingManager, baseToken, maintainer, keeperContract, and watcher, but does NOT check _emergency and _maintainerWithoutTimelock. threats.title: BalancerFlashLoan.receiveFlashLoan Allows Arbitrary External Calls to Unvalidated Destination Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: In receiveFlashLoan, the `destinationConnector[]` addresses are called with arbitrary calldata and gas limits without any validation against the registry. Only the `receiver` address is checked as an active connector. The keeper (via multisig) can direct flash-loaned funds to any contract, bypassing connector-specific safety checks and potentially interacting with unaudited or malicious contracts.
threats.investigation_rationale: The flash loan mechanism extends the keeper's execution authority beyond the defined connector interface. While keeper access requires multisig compromise (LOW realism), the lack of target validation means a compromised keeper can execute arbitrary contract interactions using flash-loaned capital.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (bool success,) = destinationConnector[i].call{ value: 0, gas: gas[i] }(callingData[i]); - destinationConnector[i] is from userData, never validated against registry

preconditions: Caller stored in BalancerFlashLoan must be the keeperContract
Receiver must be an active connector in the registry
Keeper multisig must sign the flash loan transaction (requires threshold signatures)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Arbitrary .call to unvalidated addresses with user-supplied calldata
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: destinationConnector array decoded from userData is never checked: `(bool success,) = destinationConnector[i].call{...}(callingData[i])` where destinationConnector[i] can be ANY address

threats.title: OmnichainLogic bridge approvals never expire, enabling delayed execution under changed conditions

threats.title: Gearboxv3.executeCommands restricts multicall targets to facade, but still allows dangerous facade operations; insufficient command allowlisting may enable unsafe state transitions
threats.hypothesis_description: Gearboxv3.executeCommands only checks that each MultiCall target equals the provided facade address, but does not restrict which facade functions may be invoked. If a malicious/compromised facade is configured (or if facade exposes methods that can move funds/permissions in unexpected ways), the keeper can execute arbitrary facade actions that may bypass intended connector-level safety invariants (e.g., enabling/disabling tokens, swapping collateral, changing leverage) without additional validation. While this requires manager authority or misconfiguration, it is operationally relevant given broad strategy execution capabilities.
threats.investigation_rationale: The connector attempts to constrain call targets but not call semantics. In production, unsafe multicalls are a common source of loss when combined with a permissive strategy engine and external protocol changes.

operational_questions: Does UNIv3Connector/PancakeswapConnector TVL logic read per-NFT state or aggregated pool state, and does it depend on NFT ownership?
Are there accounting manager safeguards if a connector TVL calculation fails or returns 0?
Are registry holding positions updated to reflect staking location/state?
validation_requirements: Integration test: open UNIv3 position, stake it, then call TVLHelper.getTVL and connector management methods
Check whether positionManager.collect/decreaseLiquidity require NFT ownership (expected yes)
Determine whether MasterChef exposes position value in a way used by _getPositionTVL (it does not here)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Ensure a vault strategy stakes NFTs via sendPositionToMasterChef
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Trigger share issuance/redemption around TVL mispricing windows (if any)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Exploit any mismatch to extract value (depends on AccountingManager share price mechanics)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Withdraw position from MasterChef to restore normal operations
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: High; depends on keeper actions and share accounting details

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inability to manage/close UniV3 positions while staked; TVL misreporting
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium (requires withdrawing NFT and reconciling accounting)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: LP strategies using Pancakeswap MasterChef staking

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: during periods of staking and active share operations

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: NFT transfers from connector to MasterChef; concurrent TVL anomalies
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed collect/decrease/burn attempts; discrepancies in reported vs expected LP value
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Share issuance/redemption clustered around staking events
